title: Best Mac Mini for Running Llama 3 70B: M4 vs M4 Pro Guide (2026)
headline: Best Mac Mini for Llama 3 70B in 2026
description: Which Mac Mini can actually run Llama 3 70B locally? We compare M4 vs M4 Pro unified memory configs, real inference speeds, and pricing.
intro: Running Llama 3 70B locally requires serious hardware — specifically, enough memory to hold the model weights. The Mac Mini's unified memory architecture makes it one of the few compact machines capable of this feat, but only if you choose the right configuration. This guide breaks down exactly which Mac Mini model you need, what inference speeds to expect, and whether the premium for M4 Pro is worth it.

content.html: TL;DR: To run Llama 3 70B locally, you need the Mac Mini M4 Pro with 64GB unified memory (custom order). The base M4 with 16GB cannot run 70B models at all. The M4 Pro with 24GB can technically load a heavily quantized Q4 version, but you'll be swapping to disk constantly. For smooth 70B inference at 8-12 tokens/second, 64GB is the minimum — and the M4 Pro's 273 GB/s memory bandwidth makes it viable.

content.text: Why Llama 3 70B Needs So Much Memory

content.html: Llama 3 70B has 70 billion parameters. At full precision (FP16), that's roughly 140GB of VRAM — far beyond any consumer GPU. Even with aggressive Q4 quantization (4-bit weights), you're looking at approximately 35-40GB of memory just for the model weights, plus additional overhead for context window, KV cache, and the inference runtime itself. This is why most PC users need dual RTX 4090s ($3,200+ in GPUs alone) to run 70B models comfortably.

Apple Silicon changes this equation. The Mac Mini M4 Pro uses unified memory — the same RAM pool is shared between CPU and GPU. There's no separate VRAM limitation. If you configure 64GB of unified memory, all 64GB is available for your LLM. This architectural advantage makes the Mac Mini one of the most cost-effective ways to run 70B models locally, despite Apple's premium pricing.

Not all Mac Minis are created equal for LLM workloads. The base Mac Mini M4 maxes out at 16GB (or 24GB with upgrade), while the M4 Pro can be configured up to 64GB. Memory bandwidth also differs significantly — and bandwidth directly impacts tokens per second during inference.

Critical: Mac Mini memory is soldered and not upgradeable after purchase. If you think you might run 70B models, order the 64GB configuration upfront. There's no $400 RAM upgrade path later — you'd need to sell and rebuy.

content.text: M4 vs M4 Pro: Core Specs That Matter for LLMs

content.html: Beyond memory capacity, the M4 and M4 Pro chips differ in ways that directly impact inference performance. The M4 Pro has more CPU cores (14 vs 10), more GPU cores (20 vs 10), and crucially, more than double the memory bandwidth (273 GB/s vs 120 GB/s). For LLM inference, memory bandwidth is often the bottleneck — the GPU spends most of its time waiting for weights to load from memory.

The 2.3x bandwidth advantage of the M4 Pro translates directly to faster inference. At 7B model sizes, you're looking at 65 tokens/second on M4 Pro vs 42 t/s on M4. This gap widens with larger models because memory bandwidth becomes increasingly critical as model size grows. For 70B specifically, the M4 Pro's bandwidth is what makes the difference between 'technically runs' and 'actually usable.'

Let's be direct about methodology: Apple doesn't publish official LLM benchmarks, and real-world performance varies based on quantization method, context length, and inference backend. Our estimates for 70B models (8-12 t/s on 64GB M4 Pro) are extrapolated from the confirmed 7B and 13B performance data in Apple's spec sheets, combined with the known relationship between model size and memory bandwidth requirements.

The formula is straightforward: inference speed is roughly proportional to memory_bandwidth / model_size_in_memory. A Q4-quantized Llama 3 70B consumes approximately 35-40GB. With 273 GB/s bandwidth, you can theoretically cycle through those weights 7-8 times per second — but overhead from KV cache, attention computation, and the inference stack reduces this. Real-world performance lands around 8-12 tokens/second for single-batch inference using llama.cpp or Ollama on macOS.

Context length matters: These estimates assume 2K-4K context windows. At 8K+ context, KV cache memory requirements grow significantly, and you may see inference speeds drop to 5-8 t/s as memory pressure increases. For long-context use cases, 64GB becomes even more critical.

content.text: Quantization Methods: Q4 vs Q5 vs Q8

content.html: When running Llama 3 70B on consumer hardware, you'll be using quantized models — reduced precision weights that trade some quality for dramatically lower memory requirements. The most common formats are GGUF files optimized for llama.cpp, which Ollama uses under the hood. Here's what each quantization level means for 70B models:

items: Q4_K_M (4-bit): ~35-40GB memory, fastest inference, slight quality loss on complex reasoning
Q5_K_M (5-bit): ~45-50GB memory, better quality, requires 64GB config with tight margins
Q6_K (6-bit): ~55GB memory, near-FP16 quality, 64GB config will swap slightly
Q8_0 (8-bit): ~70GB memory, requires >64GB — not viable on any Mac Mini
FP16 (full): ~140GB memory, not possible on consumer hardware

content.html: For the 64GB Mac Mini M4 Pro, Q4_K_M is the sweet spot. You get comfortable headroom for context window and system overhead, while maintaining inference speeds in the 8-12 t/s range. Q5_K_M is possible but leaves little margin — expect occasional slowdowns if you push context length. Avoid Q6 and higher on 64GB; you'll spend more time swapping than inferring.

content.text: Setting Up Llama 3 70B on Mac Mini

content.html: Getting Llama 3 70B running on your Mac Mini M4 Pro is straightforward thanks to Ollama's native Apple Silicon support. Here's the exact process, assuming you have a 64GB M4 Pro configuration:

items: Download and install Ollama from ollama.ai — the macOS installer handles everything
Open Terminal and run: ollama pull llama3:70b-instruct-q4_K_M
Wait for the ~40GB download to complete (ensure you have storage space)
Run: ollama run llama3:70b-instruct-q4_K_M
Start chatting — first response takes 10-15 seconds as the model loads into memory

content.html: For power users, llama.cpp offers more control over inference parameters. Install via Homebrew (brew install llama.cpp), download GGUF weights from HuggingFace, and run with custom context lengths and batch sizes. The --mlock flag prevents macOS from swapping model weights to disk, which is critical for consistent performance.

Pro tip: Enable 'Reduce Memory' mode in Ollama settings if you're running other apps alongside your LLM. This trades some inference speed for better system responsiveness. For dedicated inference servers, disable this and let the model claim all available memory.
Llama 3 70B isn't the only large model worth running locally. Depending on your use case, alternatives may offer better performance or capabilities at similar memory requirements:

items: Llama 3.1 70B: Updated version with better instruction following and coding ability — same memory footprint
Mixtral 8x22B (MoE): Mixture-of-experts architecture, only loads ~45B parameters per inference — faster than dense 70B
Qwen 2 72B: Strong multilingual performance, particularly Chinese — similar Q4 memory requirements
DeepSeek-V2 70B: Excellent code generation, efficient MoE design — worth testing if coding is primary use
Llama 2 70B: Previous generation, noticeably weaker than Llama 3 — only use if you need specific fine-tunes

content.html: For most users, Llama 3 70B or 3.1 70B remains the best general-purpose choice at this parameter count. The quality improvements over Llama 2 are substantial — better reasoning, fewer hallucinations, and stronger instruction following. Mixtral 8x22B is worth exploring if you need faster responses and can accept slightly lower quality on some tasks.

content.text: Who Should NOT Buy a Mac Mini for 70B Models

content.html: The Mac Mini M4 Pro is excellent for local LLM inference, but it's not the right choice for everyone. Here's who should look elsewhere:

items: Budget-constrained users: At $1,999+ for the 64GB config, you can build a used dual-GPU PC for less
CUDA-dependent workflows: If your pipeline requires PyTorch with CUDA, TensorRT, or NVIDIA-specific tools, macOS won't work
Stable Diffusion power users: The 20 GPU cores are adequate but not competitive with RTX 4080/4090 for image generation throughput
Users who need >64GB: Some workflows (very long context, multiple models) exceed 64GB — you need a workstation
Batch inference at scale: For processing thousands of requests, cloud APIs or dedicated GPU servers are more cost-effective

content.html: If you fall into these categories, consider alternatives: a used RTX 3090 build ($800-1000) handles 70B with 24GB VRAM and aggressive quantization; dual RTX 4090 setups offer superior throughput for production workloads; or simply use cloud APIs (Groq, Together, Fireworks) if you don't need offline capability. The Mac Mini shines for personal use, always-on home servers, and users already in the Apple ecosystem.

content.text: Cost Analysis: Mac Mini vs PC Build vs Cloud

content.html: Let's compare the total cost of ownership for running Llama 3 70B across different approaches. The Mac Mini M4 Pro 64GB costs approximately $1,999. A PC capable of similar performance requires either dual RTX 3090s (used, ~$1,600 total for GPUs) plus a compatible motherboard, PSU, and case (~$500), or a single RTX 4090 with aggressive quantization (less headroom). Electricity costs also differ significantly.

The Mac Mini's 30W power consumption is its hidden advantage. Running 24/7, it costs roughly $2/month in electricity — a PC with dual GPUs costs 10-25x more to operate. Over a 3-year ownership period, the Mac Mini often becomes the cheapest option for heavy users who would otherwise rack up significant cloud API bills or PC electricity costs.

content.text: Verdict: The 64GB M4 Pro Is Your Only Real Option

content.html: If your goal is specifically to run Llama 3 70B on a Mac Mini, you have exactly one viable path: the Mac Mini M4 Pro configured with 64GB unified memory. The base 24GB M4 Pro can technically load a Q4 model, but performance is crippled by constant disk swapping — you'll get 2-4 tokens/second at best, making it unusable for interactive chat. The base M4 models are completely ruled out; 16-24GB simply cannot hold a 70B model in any quantization format.

At $1,999 for the 64GB configuration, the Mac Mini M4 Pro represents the most power-efficient and compact way to run 70B models locally. You won't match the raw throughput of a $3,000+ dual-GPU PC build, but you'll get silent operation, macOS compatibility, a 5-inch form factor, and electricity bills under $3/month. For home users, developers, and researchers who want local 70B inference without building a PC, this is the clear recommendation.

Final recommendation: Order the Mac Mini M4 Pro with 64GB unified memory. Don't try to save money with 24GB — you'll regret it immediately. The $600 upgrade from 24GB to 64GB is the most important money you'll spend if 70B models are your target. For smaller models (7B-13B), the base M4 at $599 is excellent value, but it's a completely different use case.

faq.question: Can the base Mac Mini M4 with 16GB run Llama 3 70B?
faq.answer: No. Llama 3 70B requires approximately 35-40GB of memory even with aggressive Q4 quantization. The base Mac Mini M4's 16GB unified memory can only handle models up to 13B parameters. For 70B, you need the Mac Mini M4 Pro with 64GB — the 24GB configurations are technically possible but result in severe performance degradation from disk swapping.

faq.question: What inference speed can I expect running Llama 3 70B on Mac Mini M4 Pro 64GB?
faq.answer: With Q4_K_M quantization and 2K-4K context windows, expect approximately 8-12 tokens per second for single-batch inference using Ollama or llama.cpp. This is slower than dual RTX 4090 setups (15-20 t/s) but fast enough for interactive chat. Longer context windows (8K+) will reduce speeds to 5-8 t/s due to increased KV cache memory pressure.

faq.question: Is the Mac Mini M4 Pro 24GB enough for Llama 3 70B?
faq.answer: Technically yes, practically no. A Q4-quantized Llama 3 70B needs ~35-40GB. With only 24GB unified memory, macOS will constantly swap model weights between memory and SSD, resulting in 2-4 tokens/second — too slow for interactive use. Spend the extra $600 for 64GB if 70B is your goal.

faq.question: How does Mac Mini M4 Pro compare to RTX 4090 for running Llama 3 70B?
faq.answer: A single RTX 4090 has 24GB VRAM, which cannot hold Llama 3 70B even quantized (needs 35-40GB). You'd need dual RTX 4090s ($3,200+ in GPUs) to match the Mac Mini M4 Pro 64GB ($1,999) in memory capacity. The dual 4090 setup offers faster inference (15-20 t/s vs 8-12 t/s) but costs more, consumes 700W+ under load, and requires a full PC build.

faq.question: What quantization should I use for Llama 3 70B on Mac Mini?
faq.answer: Q4_K_M is the optimal choice for 64GB Mac Mini M4 Pro. It uses ~35-40GB memory, leaving headroom for context window and system processes. Q5_K_M offers slightly better quality but pushes memory limits. Avoid Q6 and Q8 quantizations — they exceed comfortable memory margins and cause swapping.

faq.question: Can I upgrade Mac Mini memory later to run larger models?
faq.answer: No. Mac Mini memory is soldered to the logic board and cannot be upgraded after purchase. If you anticipate running 70B models or want future flexibility, you must order the 64GB configuration upfront. Buying a 24GB model with plans to upgrade later is not possible — you'd need to sell and repurchase.

faq.question: How much does it cost to run Mac Mini M4 Pro 24/7 for LLM inference?
faq.answer: The Mac Mini M4 Pro consumes approximately 30W under sustained inference load. Running 24/7, that's roughly 21.6 kWh per month. At the US average electricity rate (~$0.10/kWh), expect about $2.16/month. This is 10-25x cheaper than running a dual-GPU PC setup for the same workload.

faq.question: Does Mac Mini support CUDA for running LLMs?
faq.answer: No. CUDA is NVIDIA-proprietary and only runs on NVIDIA GPUs. Mac Mini uses Apple Silicon, which relies on Metal for GPU compute. However, popular LLM tools like Ollama and llama.cpp have native Apple Silicon support with Metal acceleration — you don't need CUDA for local inference. PyTorch also supports MPS (Metal Performance Shaders) backend on macOS.
